author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text:
I can understand the geometry of an airplane wing shape as from root to tip it is all meeting the air at the same velocity (I'm ignoring the mechanics/geometry of weight/lift/strength of modern wing). But with a rotating 'wing' i.e. turbine or propeller, the tip is moving fastest, the root hardly at all. However, a turbine blade is applying torque to the axle, and the leverage effect means its most effective with force applied at the tip. So why aren't the blades much wider at the tip to 'catch' most air? (I'm thinking something like the multi-blade windmills of the old american ranch waterpump windmills) Can someone explain this in clear terms?
Even if the tubine blade/wing has an extended tip (to reduce the effect of vortex shedding on the rest of the blade) there must be a sweet-spot along the blade where widest is most efficient to extract maximum torque-effect from the airstream.

author: @salilsahani2721

author: @AndreasEisele
text: I assume that any wind turbine, when trying to maximise the energy it collects, will run at some rotational speed that is proportional to the wind speed. OTOH, as you explained in a very convincing way, the energy that can be extracted grows with the cube of the wind speed. From these two we can assume that the torque or the mechanical load that optimises the efficiency of any given turbine will have to grow with the square of the speed. Let's say you want to store the energy mechanically by lifting a (constant) weight. You would need some continuously variable transmission system that makes sure that the gear ratio grows with the square of the wind speed. I can imagine one can build such a transmission system, but how to do it in a simple and robust way is an interesting challenge in itself.

author: @danedmiston9673
text: The Betz power coefficient limit of .59 often referenced.  The less known "Whirlpool losses Schmitz power coefficient as a function of the tip speed ratio" curve is very useful for estimating the efficiency of a wind turbine.  The Schmitz curve shows the maximum theoretical power that can obtained at given tip speed ratio, TSR.  A wind turbine operating at a TSR of 0.5 has a maximum power coefficient of only .238, at a TSR of 1.0 it increases to .400, and at a TSR of 10.0 it nears the Betz limit at .572.

author: @ridekernow
text: I’ve always struggled to understand why all modern turbines are 3 bladed. The ideal you presented here effectively calculates the power output based on a “wall” of wind hitting the blades. Once it’s passed through, it’s gone. Energy dissipated. Converted. Kaput. End of. So why not put 6 (or 9..or 12) blades to capture the power from that wall of wind - and max it out? There is a huge amount of energy in the wind that must just pass straight through the three turbine blades, uncollected.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
Struuggling isn't good, working things outt.. is much better.
(Work with "Less is more")

More blades interfere with the wind - upstream of the turbine, more blades increase mechanical complexity, more blades increase cost, more blades increase maintenance
Fewer blades induce increased plasticity in the rotation, fewer blades increase "tower effect", fewer blades increase bearing stress. Fewer blades reduce power output (for any given rotor diameter), fewer blades reduce cost/maintenance /complexity - intil there is only 1 and those things Likely increase again..
Even numbers of blades induce harmonic oscillation into the whole system.

Pick you optimal number, more than 2 and likely less than or equal to 5, with an off (preferably prime) number dominating.

The lower the number the less disruptive hub interactions and the lower the drag when stopped...

(Not saying you are incorrect, but the haters look to Holland or Greece with their old "4 bladers" as something of a marvel in contrast... - romantics often disconnect intellect.)

author: @jsincoherency
text: How does the Betz limit apply if you were to stack a bunch of separate turbines in front of one another? i.e. the first turbine slows the air down by 60% and extracts the energy, but then the next turbine can slow down that resulting airflow by 60%, and the next one can slow down what's left of that by 60%? Does this allow you to get arbitrarily close to 100% efficiency, or is there still some magic that means the system as a whole must obey the Betz limit even if you or I would recognise them as multiple individual turbines?

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
- the best you get is a very expensive marginally performing system - when just 2x the wind turbines separated adequately give 2x  (ie 1 + 1  - maths is dead easy ) 60% (theoretical limit) - 2 stacked can only give 60%+(60% of 60%).....(excetera for 'n' numbers of repeats )- lots more material, not much energy - worshiping efficiency gets wasteful quickly - call it "diminishing return").  lol.

The only way to beat Betz is to change the "critical area" - either using an upwind "collector" or a downwind "diffuser"  - or some combination which doesn't use too much material, disrupt the flow through the blades (air just "flows around" if you try to force the pressure to rise too much) , or cause the tower cost to increase astronomically - high winds and breakage are a thing)

replies.author: @jsincoherency
replies.text: @kadmow I'm aware the second turbine would be getting 60% of the 40% that remains after the first turbine has extracted some energy. That's not my issue. But if the Betz limit says the maximum proportion of the energy you can extract from the air flow is 60% in total, but stacking multiple turbines allows you to exceed 60%, then it doesn't seem like much of a limit at all, because whether you consider a system to be "2 turbines consecutively" or "1 big turbine system" is just a matter of opinion. Which makes me think that maybe there is some reason that the second turbing actually *wouldn't* be able to get 60% of the remaining 40%. Perhaps because the restriction it places on the "exhaust" of the first turbine means the first turbine's efficiency goes down to compensate? I don't know.

author: @DonXisco
text: Thanks for this talk... one of the issues I've always had with wind turbine math is that it always assumes the air is moving in a laminar flow like in the picture at 5:15.  This is not how nature works or how air flows with its particles swirling and spriraling (think dust devil).  That is why I especially like the tesla turbine idea with the hyperbolic diflectors, it seems to induce a vortex out of the chaotic particle movement, similar to how some water vortex turbines work.  In the apple/grass analogy, it's like it's assumed the apple hits the grass flush, vs it striking the blade at an angle which would be trasfering a lot less energy.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Great video 😊

On tip speed ratio. Yes it is simply ratio of speed of the tip of a turbine over the speed if the air. Furthermore, the pitch of the blade determins the most efficent tip speed ratio for that turbine. 

It is like this for the turbibe in order of too fast to too slow in a given wind:
1)  if your turbine spins too fast in a given wibd,your turbibe acts like a fan speeding up the wind. 
2) Slow the turbine down some and at a point the blades will simply cut the air neuther putting energy into the wind nor pulling any energy out.
3) if you slow the turbine further you extract more and more energy out of the wind  in an upward curve untill you reach a maximum amount of energy that the turbine may extract  from the available power. After that,
4) slowing the turbine further reduces the amount of wind energy converted to rotational energy more and more untill the wind turbine is not spinning and there is zero energy transfered from the wind to rotate a non rotating turbine. 😊

author: @PetraKann
text:
The power output is proportional to the Air Speed cubed.
This is why the location of Wind Turbines is so important.
If the average wind speed is say 5 m/s and it doubles to 10 m/s the power output goes by a factor of 8 (ie 2 cubed which is equal to 8).
Find a windy location that has on average double the air speed of your current location, and your power output increases by 8 fold (or 800% - all other factors being equal).
The power output is also proportional to the blade length squared. So doubling the length of the blade will generate 4 times the power.
The Coefficient of Performance is the tricky factor to determine. It's usually calculated empirically, especially with exotic blade designs.
It is common to see three blades on large wind turbines. And there is good reason for this if you examine the performance and power curves.The theoretical upper limit for Turbine efficiency is approximately 16/27 of about 60%.
There are many energy losses along the Turbine power generation train....cant do much about this apart from minimising these losses.

replies.author: @UncleKennysPlace
replies.text: If lift (airplane wing) increases with the air speed squared, why does it become cubed for wind turbines?

replies.author: @PetraKann
replies.text:
@UncleKennysPlace One is a foil whilst the other is a directed blade. Lift is largely driven by a pressure differential - the Bernoulli effect. Other factors involved too.
If you turned the aeroplane 90 degrees and had the wings facing directly into the wind, it would not be a squared relationship.
The plane would not fly either.
Good question.

Is the lift formula different for supersonic speeds?

replies.author: @danedmiston9673
replies.text: @UncleKennysPlace The lift squares the torque times twice the speed equal eight times the power.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
@UncleKennysPlace  -  We are working out the Power (using an energy formula) Watts come out of  "Instantaneous" Joules (J)... (KE = 1/2 m v^2)

(In Aerodynamics "similar equations" are usually working out the Force (Newtons - N) - ie Drag Force (Mass  * Acceleration)   (F = m a) - the "1/2 rho v^2" is the dynamic pressure....

Kinetic Energy normally has mass and velocity squared, in the equation,  wind Equations have density and volume (sectional area multiplied by  "unit" length)  -  the axial measurement for the "air volume" is determined by the "second of travel" - unit of time (second), therefore the velocity of the wind gets input to compute the "effective volume (mass) of air". (m = cp rho A v)
 - Power is the time derivative of Energy - ie the The seconds worth of Watts  is the Joules  (at 1 second increments these things are "Equivalent" - for doing math)

So the v^3 is lumped at the tail end of the equation.  (a working understanding of the mechanics is better than rabbiting formulas)
